runs.interpretation: Primary-earner substitution-response elasticity for earnings decile 5 in a U.S. microsimulation model under current-law tax-benefit rules.
citations: Saez, Slemrod, and Giertz (2012) review of taxable-income elasticities
Heckman and Meyer-style labor supply elasticity estimates for prime-age men
Tax-benefit microsimulation practice using modest primary-earner labor-supply responses
runs.reasoningSummary: I think the relevant decile-5 primary earner response is modest but positive, closer to the lower end of labor-supply elasticities for primary earners than to extensive-margin estimates for secondary earners. In a current-law microsimulation context, I would center it around 0.2 with substantial uncertainty but most mass below 0.4.

runs.interpretation: Primary-earner substitution-response elasticity for earnings decile 5 in a U.S. microsimulation model under current-law tax-benefit policy.
citations: Saez, Slemrod, and Giertz (2012) review of taxable-income elasticities
Mankiw and Weinzierd (2009) on labor supply responses to taxation
IMPLAN/secondary-earner-style microsimulation conventions from public finance modeling
runs.reasoningSummary: For prime-age/primary earners in a middle earnings decile, I expect a modest but nontrivial intensive-margin response: larger than zero because current-law tax wedges matter, but well below one because hours and effort are constrained. runs.interpretation: Primary-earner substitution-response elasticity for earnings decile 5 in a U.S. microsimulation model under current-law tax-benefit simulation.
citations: Saez, E. (2002) optimal income taxation / taxable-income elasticity discussions
Blundell and MaCurdy (1999) labor supply elasticity survey
Heim and Lurie (2010s) married women labor supply responses in tax simulations
runs.reasoningSummary: For a middle earnings decile of primary earners in a U.S. tax-benefit microsimulation, I expect a modest positive substitution elasticity, smaller than values often discussed for secondary earners and larger than near-zero estimates for many prime-age men. I center on 0.25 with a fairly wide right-skewed uncertainty range because simulated effective marginal wage changes and decile-specific heterogeneity can raise responsiveness, but institutional constraints and income effects limit it.

runs.reasoningSummary: For prime/primary earners in the middle earnings decile, I expect a modest but nontrivial intensive-margin response: lower than for secondary earners or low-income extensive-margin groups, but not near zero.
